texts.label: text
texts.orig: The molecular-sieve DDR type zeolite membrane with an aperture of 0.36 · 0.44 nm was formed on a porous alumina substrate by hydrothermal process. The permeance through the membrane in the single gas feed of helium, hydrogen, carbon dioxide, oxygen, nitrogen, methane, n -butane, i -butane and sulfur hexafluoride were measured at 301 and 373 K up to 0.5 MPa. The permeance decreased by more than three orders of magnitude between 0.35 and 0.40 nm of the kinetic diameter of permeated gas at both 301 and 373 K. The separation factor of CO2 to CH4 in 50% CO2 and 50% CH4 mixed gas feed was 220 and 100 at 301 and 373 K, respectively at 0.5 MPa total gas feed pressure. The permeation properties were slightly affected by the water addition at 301 K. We conclude that the DDR type membrane has few defects and worked as molecular-sieving membrane. Ó 2003 Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.
texts.text: The molecular-sieve DDR type zeolite membrane with an aperture of 0.36 · 0.44 nm was formed on a porous alumina substrate by hydrothermal process. The permeance through the membrane in the single gas feed of helium, hydrogen, carbon dioxide, oxygen, nitrogen, methane, n -butane, i -butane and sulfur hexafluoride were measured at 301 and 373 K up to 0.5 MPa. The permeance decreased by more than three orders of magnitude between 0.35 and 0.40 nm of the kinetic diameter of permeated gas at both 301 and 373 K. The separation factor of CO2 to CH4 in 50% CO2 and 50% CH4 mixed gas feed was 220 and 100 at 301 and 373 K, respectively at 0.5 MPa total gas feed pressure. The permeation properties were slightly affected by the water addition at 301 K. We conclude that the DDR type membrane has few defects and worked as molecular-sieving membrane. Ó 2003 Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For years, gas separation through a membrane has been hoped to be an environmentally benign and simple process in refinery, chemical and petrochemical industries since distillation, extraction, absorption and adsorption processes are generally energy consuming and/or require complicated facilities. Organic membranes have been used with limited applications because of their low thermal and chemical stability. Recently the molecular-sieve inorganic membrane has been studied extensively.
texts.text: For years, gas separation through a membrane has been hoped to be an environmentally benign and simple process in refinery, chemical and petrochemical industries since distillation, extraction, absorption and adsorption processes are generally energy consuming and/or require complicated facilities. Organic membranes have been used with limited applications because of their low thermal and chemical stability. Recently the molecular-sieve inorganic membrane has been studied extensively.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: One of the attractive applications of this type of membrane is the separation of small gas molecules such as carbon dioxide (CO2) and methane (CH4). The digestive gas from the anaerobic treatment of biomass contains CH4 and CO2. Natural gas from wells often contains fairly high amounts of CO2. Both applications require the removal of CO2 to utilize enriched CH4 or natural gas as a fuel.
texts.text: One of the attractive applications of this type of membrane is the separation of small gas molecules such as carbon dioxide (CO2) and methane (CH4). The digestive gas from the anaerobic treatment of biomass contains CH4 and CO2. Natural gas from wells often contains fairly high amounts of CO2. Both applications require the removal of CO2 to utilize enriched CH4 or natural gas as a fuel.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Many authors have reported the separation of CO2 from CH4 of an equi-molar mixed feed gas with various zeolites and other inorganic microporous membranes.
texts.text: Many authors have reported the separation of CO2 from CH4 of an equi-molar mixed feed gas with various zeolites and other inorganic microporous membranes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Kusakabe et al. [1] reported a CO2/CH4 separation factor of 20 at 303 K with a FAU type zeolite membrane, ratio Si/Al ¼ 5. Weh et al. [2] showed a CO2/CH4 separation factor of 0.3 at 296K with a FAU type zeolite with a Si/Al ratio ¼ 1.3-1.8. Kusakabe et al. suggested that the selective adsorption of CO2 to Al site is critical to the separation. Lovallo et al. [3] reported a CO2/CH4 separation factor of 16at 393 K and 5.7 at 453 K with a MFI type zeolite membrane, ratio Si/Al ¼ 23. Van den Broeke et al. [4] showed a CO2/CH4 separation factor of 2.4 at 303 K and 8.5 at 225 K, and Poshusta et al. [5] reported a CO2/CH4 separation factor of 5.5 at 300 K. The separation factor is relatively low partly because the FAU and MFI type zeolite has pores larger than 0.5 nm in diameter and are too large to separate CO2 from CH4 with high selectivity.
texts.text: Kusakabe et al. [1] reported a CO2/CH4 separation factor of 20 at 303 K with a FAU type zeolite membrane, ratio Si/Al ¼ 5. Weh et al. [2] showed a CO2/CH4 separation factor of 0.3 at 296K with a FAU type zeolite with a Si/Al ratio ¼ 1.3-1.8. Kusakabe et al. suggested that the selective adsorption of CO2 to Al site is critical to the separation. Lovallo et al. [3] reported a CO2/CH4 separation factor of 16at 393 K and 5.7 at 453 K with a MFI type zeolite membrane, ratio Si/Al ¼ 23. Van den Broeke et al. [4] showed a CO2/CH4 separation factor of 2.4 at 303 K and 8.5 at 225 K, and Poshusta et al. [5] reported a CO2/CH4 separation factor of 5.5 at 300 K. The separation factor is relatively low partly because the FAU and MFI type zeolite has pores larger than 0.5 nm in diameter and are too large to separate CO2 from CH4 with high selectivity.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Poshusta et al. [6,7] fabricated a SAPO-34 (silicoaluminophosphate) membrane and showed a CO2/CH4 separation factor of 30 at 300 K and 4.7 at 470 K. They also reported the effects of water content on gas permeation and suggested that adsorbed water appeared to completely block the SAPO pores, but permeation through non-SAPO pores increased with water content. Aoki et al. [8] fabricated a LTA type zeolite membrane. They reported only the single gas permeation data and the ideal separation factor of CO2/CH4 is about 0.5 at 308 K. Guan et al. [9] fabricated a ETS-4 zeolite

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (titanium silicate) membrane with a composition of Na9Si12Ti5O38(OH). They reported only the single gas permeation data and the ideal separation factor of CO2/ CH4 was less than 2. The pore size of these membranes is about 0.4 nm and the higher separation factor can be expected. However, the separation factors reported are not so large, probably because not enough defect free membrane were successfully formed.
texts.text: (titanium silicate) membrane with a composition of Na9Si12Ti5O38(OH). They reported only the single gas permeation data and the ideal separation factor of CO2/ CH4 was less than 2. The pore size of these membranes is about 0.4 nm and the higher separation factor can be expected. However, the separation factors reported are not so large, probably because not enough defect free membrane were successfully formed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: DDR type zeolite particles with an aperture of 0.36 · 0.44 nm were synthesized by Gies [10,11]. According to previous reports on zeolite particles [1216], DDR type zeolite has the potential to separate CO2 with a kinetic diameter of 0.33 nm from CH4 with that of 0.38 nm [17]. DDR type zeolite is essentially comprised of silicon and oxygen atoms, and is expected to be affected less by water adsorption. However, no DDR type zeolite membrane was previously formed. In this study, a DDR type zeolite membrane was newly formed on a porous alumina substrate by a hydrothermal process. The selective gas separation with the molecularsieve membrane is described.
texts.text: DDR type zeolite particles with an aperture of 0.36 · 0.44 nm were synthesized by Gies [10,11]. According to previous reports on zeolite particles [1216], DDR type zeolite has the potential to separate CO2 with a kinetic diameter of 0.33 nm from CH4 with that of 0.38 nm [17]. DDR type zeolite is essentially comprised of silicon and oxygen atoms, and is expected to be affected less by water adsorption. However, no DDR type zeolite membrane was previously formed. In this study, a DDR type zeolite membrane was newly formed on a porous alumina substrate by a hydrothermal process. The selective gas separation with the molecularsieve membrane is described.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1. Preparation of zeolite crystals and coatingof the substrate
texts.text: 2.1. Preparation of zeolite crystals and coatingof the substrate

texts.label: text
texts.orig: DDR zeolite crystals were prepared by the method described in the paper previously reported by den Exter et al. [12]. The synthesis mixture molar ratio of 1-adamantaneamine:silica:ethylenediamine:water ¼ 47:100: 404:11,240 was prepared as follows. 1-Adamantanamine was dissolved in ethylenediamine and water was then added rapidly. The mixture was placed in a shaking machine for 1 h. After heating to 368 K for 1 h while stirring, the mixture was cooled down in ice and the icecooled tetramethoxysilane added drop by drop while being vigorously stirred. The mixture was again heated to 368 K, while stirring until the solution became clear. The mixture was then heated in a pressure vessel at 433 K for 25 days. The crystals obtained were analysed by X-ray diffractometry and scanning electron microscopy. The crystals were pulverised and dispersed in ionized water. A porous alumina tube with 17 mm diameter and 140 mm length (mean pore size 0.6 l m) was immersed in the dispersed water and then dried in an oven at 323 K for half an hour.
texts.text: DDR zeolite crystals were prepared by the method described in the paper previously reported by den Exter et al. [12]. The synthesis mixture molar ratio of 1-adamantaneamine:silica:ethylenediamine:water ¼ 47:100: 404:11,240 was prepared as follows. 1-Adamantanamine was dissolved in ethylenediamine and water was then added rapidly. The mixture was placed in a shaking machine for 1 h. After heating to 368 K for 1 h while stirring, the mixture was cooled down in ice and the icecooled tetramethoxysilane added drop by drop while being vigorously stirred. The mixture was again heated to 368 K, while stirring until the solution became clear. The mixture was then heated in a pressure vessel at 433 K for 25 days. The crystals obtained were analysed by X-ray diffractometry and scanning electron microscopy. The crystals were pulverised and dispersed in ionized water. A porous alumina tube with 17 mm diameter and 140 mm length (mean pore size 0.6 l m) was immersed in the dispersed water and then dried in an oven at 323 K for half an hour.

texts.text: The mixture molar ratio of 1-adamantaneamine:silica:ethylenediamine:water ¼ 9:100:150:4000 was prepared in a perfluoro container. The porous alumina tube with zeolite crystals as a seed was immersed in the mixture. The mixture was heated in a pressure vessel at

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 423 K for 48 h. Then, the membrane was calcined at 973 K for 5 h. The DDR type zeolite membrane was formed on the porous alumina substrate. The membrane was analysed by X-ray diffractometry. The surface and the cross-section of the membrane were observed by scanning electron microscopy. The ratio of Si to Al of the zeolite membrane was analysed from the membrane surface by electron probe microanalysis.
texts.text: 423 K for 48 h. Then, the membrane was calcined at 973 K for 5 h. The DDR type zeolite membrane was formed on the porous alumina substrate. The membrane was analysed by X-ray diffractometry. The surface and the cross-section of the membrane were observed by scanning electron microscopy. The ratio of Si to Al of the zeolite membrane was analysed from the membrane surface by electron probe microanalysis.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The permeated flux through the membrane was measured by constant pressure method and sweep gas method. A single gas of helium, hydrogen, CO2, oxygen, nitrogen and CH4, and a mixed gas of 50% CO2 and 50% CH4 was fed outside of the zeolite membrane and the permeated flux through the membrane was measured with a soap film flow meter by constant pressure method. The effect of permeated flux on the feed gas absolute pressure was examined between 0.2 and 0.5 MPa at 301 and 373 K. The permeated side was kept at atmospheric pressure. In the case of the mixed gas feed, the methane content in the permeated side was also analysed by gas chromatography.
texts.text: The permeated flux through the membrane was measured by constant pressure method and sweep gas method. A single gas of helium, hydrogen, CO2, oxygen, nitrogen and CH4, and a mixed gas of 50% CO2 and 50% CH4 was fed outside of the zeolite membrane and the permeated flux through the membrane was measured with a soap film flow meter by constant pressure method. The effect of permeated flux on the feed gas absolute pressure was examined between 0.2 and 0.5 MPa at 301 and 373 K. The permeated side was kept at atmospheric pressure. In the case of the mixed gas feed, the methane content in the permeated side was also analysed by gas chromatography.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The single gas of n -butane, i -butane and sulfur hexafluoride was measured by sweep gas method because the permeated flux was too low to measure with a soap film flow meter. The feed gas absolute pressure was 0.5 MPa at 301 and 373 K. The helium sweep gas was fed inside the membrane at atmospheric pressure at a flow rate of 2.2 mmol/min and the permeated gas concentration in the sweep gas was analysed by gas chromatography.
texts.text: The single gas of n -butane, i -butane and sulfur hexafluoride was measured by sweep gas method because the permeated flux was too low to measure with a soap film flow meter. The feed gas absolute pressure was 0.5 MPa at 301 and 373 K. The helium sweep gas was fed inside the membrane at atmospheric pressure at a flow rate of 2.2 mmol/min and the permeated gas concentration in the sweep gas was analysed by gas chromatography.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Water was added to a mixed feed gas of 50% CO2 and 50% CH4 at atmospheric pressure by bubbling the water at room temperature. The water content of the feed gas was about 3.3%. The permeated flux was measured with a soap film meter by sweep gas method.
texts.text: Water was added to a mixed feed gas of 50% CO2 and 50% CH4 at atmospheric pressure by bubbling the water at room temperature. The water content of the feed gas was about 3.3%. The permeated flux was measured with a soap film meter by sweep gas method.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: DDR crystals are shown in Fig. 1. The size of the crystals ranged around 0.1 l m, but also included crystals larger than 1 l m. The surface and the cross-section of the membrane are shown in Fig. 2. The dense membrane was formed uniformly and the thickness of the membrane was 5-10 l m. The X-ray diffraction patterns of the DDR crystals and DDR membrane are shown in Fig. 3. Both diffraction patterns indicate the crystal structure of a DDR type zeolite. It is suggested from the diffraction pattern of the membrane that the membrane was rarely oriented. The ratio of Si to Al of zeolite membrane was 980.
texts.text: DDR crystals are shown in Fig. 1. The size of the crystals ranged around 0.1 l m, but also included crystals larger than 1 l m. The surface and the cross-section of the membrane are shown in Fig. 2. The dense membrane was formed uniformly and the thickness of the membrane was 5-10 l m. The X-ray diffraction patterns of the DDR crystals and DDR membrane are shown in Fig. 3. Both diffraction patterns indicate the crystal structure of a DDR type zeolite. It is suggested from the diffraction pattern of the membrane that the membrane was rarely oriented. The ratio of Si to Al of zeolite membrane was 980.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.2. Permeated flux of single gas feed
texts.text: 3.2. Permeated flux of single gas feed

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The effect of single gas feed pressure up to 0.5 MPa on the permeated flux at 301 and 373 K is shown in Fig. 4. The permeated flux of helium, hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen and CH4 single gas feed increased linearly with increasing feed pressure. The permeated flux of the CO2 single gas feed deviated towards smaller value with increasing feed pressure at 301 K, while increased linearly at 373 K. The permeated flux at 373 K was smaller than that at 301 K except for CH4, where the permeated flux showed similar values between 301 and 373 K.
texts.text: The effect of single gas feed pressure up to 0.5 MPa on the permeated flux at 301 and 373 K is shown in Fig. 4. The permeated flux of helium, hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen and CH4 single gas feed increased linearly with increasing feed pressure. The permeated flux of the CO2 single gas feed deviated towards smaller value with increasing feed pressure at 301 K, while increased linearly at 373 K. The permeated flux at 373 K was smaller than that at 301 K except for CH4, where the permeated flux showed similar values between 301 and 373 K.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The permeance of each gas was calculated from the slope of Fig. 4 and plotted against the kinetic diameter of various gases [17] in Fig. 5. The permeance of CO2 at 301 K was calculated at the feed pressure of 0.5 MPa. As shown in Fig. 5, the permeance decreased by more than three orders of magnitude between 0.35 and 0.40 nm of the kinetic diameter of gas at both 301 and 373 K.

The permeance of CO2 at 301 K was calculated at the feed pressure of 0.5 MPa. As shown in Fig. 5, the permeance decreased by more than three orders of magnitude between 0.35 and 0.40 nm of the kinetic diameter of gas at both 301 and 373 K.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The permeation of gas through the micropores of the zeolite can be explained qualitatively by the adsorptiondiffusion mechanism, where the permeated flux is expressed as the diffusion rate through the micropores times the difference of adsorbed gas concentrations between feed side and permeated side. The diffusion rate

The diffusion rate

texts.label: text
texts.orig: becomes significantly smaller when the kinetic diameter of gas becomes larger than the size of the zeolite micropores due to the molecular-sieve effect. Sakai et al. [18] proved that the permeated flux of p -xylene with a kinetic diameter of 0.51 nm was more than a hundred times larger than those of o - and m -xylenes with a kinetic diameter of 0.56nm through the micropores of a MFI type zeolite membrane with pore size of 0.53 · 0.56nm. The DDR type zeolite possesses an aperture of 0.36 · 0.44 nm. This suggests that the significant decrease of the permeated flux for the kinetic diameter of more than around 0.35 nm is due to the molecular-seiving effect.
texts.text: becomes significantly smaller when the kinetic diameter of gas becomes larger than the size of the zeolite micropores due to the molecular-sieve effect. Sakai et al. [18] proved that the permeated flux of p -xylene with a kinetic diameter of 0.51 nm was more than a hundred times larger than those of o - and m -xylenes with a kinetic diameter of 0.56nm through the micropores of a MFI type zeolite membrane with pore size of 0.53 · 0.56nm. The DDR type zeolite possesses an aperture of 0.36 · 0.44 nm. This suggests that the significant decrease of the permeated flux for the kinetic diameter of more than around 0.35 nm is due to the molecular-seiving effect.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The highest permeance of CO2 gas was explained by the amount of adsorption. The adsorption amount of various gases to DDR type zeolite particles was reported at 296K up to 120 kPa [12]. The adsorption of hydrogen is less than 0.1 mmol/g at 120 kPa, while that of CO2 at 120 kPa is 2.0 mmol/g. CO2 adsorbed to DDR type zeolite more than hydrogen by a factor of 20, and helium is known to be inert and was the least adsorbed gas. Therefore CO2 showed the highest permeance although the kinetic diameter of CO2 is slightly larger than that of hydrogen and helium.
texts.text: The highest permeance of CO2 gas was explained by the amount of adsorption. The adsorption amount of various gases to DDR type zeolite particles was reported at 296K up to 120 kPa [12]. The adsorption of hydrogen is less than 0.1 mmol/g at 120 kPa, while that of CO2 at 120 kPa is 2.0 mmol/g. CO2 adsorbed to DDR type zeolite more than hydrogen by a factor of 20, and helium is known to be inert and was the least adsorbed gas. Therefore CO2 showed the highest permeance although the kinetic diameter of CO2 is slightly larger than that of hydrogen and helium.

texts.orig: The permeated flux of CO2 and CH4 in the mixed gas feed was plotted as a function of partial feed gas pressure in Fig. 6. The permeated flux of CH4 was magnified by a factor of 100 in Fig. 6. Since CO2 concentration in the permeated side is more than 99%, the partial pressure of CO2 in the permeated side is almost 0.1 MPa, while that of CH4 is 0.0 MPa. Therefore the straight line intercepts at 0.1 MPa for CO2, and at 0.0 MPa for CH4

texts.label: text
texts.orig: magnitude. It is indicated from the similar CO2 permeance data in the single and the mixed gas feed that CO2 molecules can pass over CH4 molecules inside the twodimensional micropore network of the DDR type zeolite. On the other hand, CH4 molecules diffuse among the flow of significantly faster CO2 molecules. It is speculated that the diffusion of CH4 molecules might be accelerated in the mixed gas feed by the collisions of faster diffusing CO2 molecules inside the micropores.
texts.text: magnitude. It is indicated from the similar CO2 permeance data in the single and the mixed gas feed that CO2 molecules can pass over CH4 molecules inside the twodimensional micropore network of the DDR type zeolite. On the other hand, CH4 molecules diffuse among the flow of significantly faster CO2 molecules. It is speculated that the diffusion of CH4 molecules might be accelerated in the mixed gas feed by the collisions of faster diffusing CO2 molecules inside the micropores.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: At 301 K the permeance of CO2 decreased from 7 · 10 � 8 mol/m 2 s Pa in the dry mixed gas feed to 4 · 10 � 8 mol/m 2 s Pa in the mixed gas feed with 3.3% water. The separation factors of dry and wet gas feed were 280 and 330, respectively. On the other hand, at 373 K the permeance was 4 · 10 � 8 mol/m 2 s Pa and the separation factor was 120. No changes were observed between dry and wet gas feed.
texts.text: At 301 K the permeance of CO2 decreased from 7 · 10 � 8 mol/m 2 s Pa in the dry mixed gas feed to 4 · 10 � 8 mol/m 2 s Pa in the mixed gas feed with 3.3% water. The separation factors of dry and wet gas feed were 280 and 330, respectively. On the other hand, at 373 K the permeance was 4 · 10 � 8 mol/m 2 s Pa and the separation factor was 120. No changes were observed between dry and wet gas feed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It is suggested that water adsorb into the micropores to block the gas permeation of both CO2 and CH4 at 301 K to some extent, while at 373 K little effect of water adsorption on the gas permeation was found. The DDR type zeolite membrane consists only of silicon and oxygen atoms and is classified as hydrophobic among other zeolites. However, the ratio of Si to Al was 980 probably because aluminum atoms were incorporated into the membrane from the substrate in the course of a hydrothermal process. It is thought that DDR type zeolite membrane is not perfectly hydrophobic and some water adsorption effects were observed at 301 K.
texts.text: It is suggested that water adsorb into the micropores to block the gas permeation of both CO2 and CH4 at 301 K to some extent, while at 373 K little effect of water adsorption on the gas permeation was found. The DDR type zeolite membrane consists only of silicon and oxygen atoms and is classified as hydrophobic among other zeolites. However, the ratio of Si to Al was 980 probably because aluminum atoms were incorporated into the membrane from the substrate in the course of a hydrothermal process. It is thought that DDR type zeolite membrane is not perfectly hydrophobic and some water adsorption effects were observed at 301 K.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: DDR type zeolite membrane with few defects was newly formed on a porous alumina substrate, and CO2 and CH4 could be selectively separated by the molecular-sieving effect with a high separation factor of 220 at 301 K.
texts.text: DDR type zeolite membrane with few defects was newly formed on a porous alumina substrate, and CO2 and CH4 could be selectively separated by the molecular-sieving effect with a high separation factor of 220 at 301 K.
